machine-learning.plain: Getting a computer to work out the rule from examples, instead of being handed the rule.
machine-learning.definition: A family of methods where a program improves at a task by adjusting itself based on data, rather than following instructions a person wrote for every case. "Learning" means: change the settings until the outputs match the examples better.

model.plain: The rule-of-thumb machine we're building — settings plus a recipe that turns an input into a prediction.
model.definition: A mathematical function with adjustable settings (parameters) that maps inputs to outputs. A straight line with its slope and offset is a model; so is a billion-parameter neural network. "Training a model" means tuning its parameters.

training.plain: The guess-grade-adjust loop: try settings, measure how wrong the guesses are, nudge the settings, repeat.
training.definition: The process of adjusting a model's parameters to reduce its loss on a set of examples. Most commonly done with gradient descent.

data-point.plain: One example: one row in your notebook, one dot on the chart.
data-point.definition: A single recorded case, usually an input (or several) together with the answer we'd like the model to produce for it. A dataset is a collection of data points.

feature.plain: One of the things we know about each example — a column in the notebook, like the day's temperature.
feature.definition: An individual measurable property used as input to a model. Real problems usually have many features per data point; choosing and preparing them well (feature engineering) often matters more than the choice of model.

label.plain: The right answer for an example — what actually happened, like that day's real sales.
label.definition: The known output value attached to a data point during training. The model's predictions are graded against the labels.

prediction.plain: The model's guess for an input it's given.
prediction.definition: The output a model produces for a given input. Written ŷ ("y-hat") to distinguish the guess from the true answer y.

parameter.plain: A setting inside the model that training is allowed to change — a knob the learning turns.
parameter.definition: A value the training process adjusts to fit the data, such as a line's slope and offset, or a neural network's millions of weights. Distinct from hyperparameters, which are set by the human before training starts.

weight.plain: How much an input matters to the prediction. For a line on a chart, it's the slope.
weight.definition: A parameter that multiplies an input. A large weight means that input strongly influences the output; zero means it's ignored; negative means it pushes the output the other way.

bias.plain: The model's starting offset: the prediction when every input is zero. For a line, where it crosses the axis.
bias.definition: A parameter added to the weighted inputs, letting the model's output start somewhere other than zero. (Unrelated to "bias" meaning unfairness in data — that's a different concept that shares the word.)

loss.plain: The model's grade: how wrong its guesses are, boiled down to one number. Lower is better, like golf.
loss.definition: A function that measures the gap between predictions and true answers across the data. Training is the search for parameter values that make the loss small. Different tasks use different losses; mean squared error is the classic one for regression.

mean-squared-error.plain: Grade a model by taking each miss, squaring it, and averaging: misses in both directions count, and big misses count extra.
mean-squared-error.definition: A loss for regression: the average of (true value − prediction)² over all data points. Squaring keeps misses above and below from cancelling out and punishes large errors disproportionately — which is also why outliers pull hard on the fit.

residual.plain: One example's miss: the gap between the real answer and the model's guess — the little vertical bar from a dot down to the line.
residual.definition: The difference y − ŷ for a single data point. Patterns in the residuals (rather than random scatter) are a sign the model is missing structure in the data.

outlier.plain: A weird dot far from all the others — a typo, a fluke, or a genuinely unusual case.
outlier.definition: A data point that lies unusually far from the overall pattern. Squared-error losses give outliers outsized influence, so handling them (investigating, capping, or using a robust loss) is a routine part of real ML work.

gradient.plain: The "which way is uphill" arrow: for each setting, does the loss go up or down if I nudge it, and how steeply?
gradient.definition: The collection of a function's derivatives with respect to each parameter — the direction of steepest increase. Training moves parameters against the gradient (downhill) to shrink the loss.

derivative.plain: How fast one thing changes when you nudge another — the mathematical answer to "if I turn this knob a tiny bit, what happens?"
derivative.definition: The rate of change of a function at a point. The partial derivative ∂L/∂w reads "how the loss L changes as the weight w changes, holding everything else still." It is the core tool that lets training know which way to adjust each parameter.

gradient-descent.plain: Learning by walking downhill: repeatedly nudge every setting in whichever direction makes the grade a little better.
gradient-descent.definition: An algorithm that iteratively updates parameters a small step against the gradient of the loss: w ← w − η·∂L/∂w. Almost all of deep learning is powered by variants of this loop (SGD, momentum, Adam).

learning-rate.plain: How big each nudge is. Too small and learning crawls; too big and it overshoots and bounces around.
learning-rate.definition: A hyperparameter multiplying the gradient in each update step. One of the first knobs to tune when training misbehaves.

linear-regression.plain: Fitting the best straight line through your dots, then using the line to predict.
linear-regression.definition: A model whose prediction is a weighted sum of the inputs plus a bias: ŷ = wx + b (one input) or ŷ = w₁x₁ + … + wₙxₙ + b (many). Usually fit by minimizing mean squared error — which has a closed-form solution, but is also the standard first example of gradient descent.

regression.plain: Predicting a number: tomorrow's temperature, a house price, how many ice creams.
regression.definition: The family of tasks where the output is a continuous quantity. Contrast with classification, where the output is a category.

classification.plain: Predicting a category: spam or not, cat or dog, which digit is in the picture.
classification.definition: The family of tasks where the output is one of a fixed set of classes. Uses different losses and metrics than regression (accuracy, precision/recall, cross-entropy).

supervised-learning.plain: Learning from worked examples: every practice question comes with the right answer attached.
supervised-learning.definition: Training on data points that include labels, so the model's guesses can be graded directly. Regression and classification are both supervised.

unsupervised-learning.plain: Learning without answer keys: find structure in the data on your own, like sorting a pile of photos into groups.
unsupervised-learning.definition: Methods that work on unlabeled data — clustering (k-means), dimensionality reduction (PCA), density estimation. The model discovers patterns rather than matching known answers.

training-set.plain: The examples the model practices on — the homework.
training-set.definition: The portion of the data used to fit the parameters. Performance here is not proof of learning: a model can ace data it memorized.

test-set.plain: Examples held back and never shown during practice — the real exam.
test-set.definition: Data kept aside to estimate how the model performs on cases it hasn't seen. Looking at it during development erodes its value; that's what a separate validation set is for.

overfitting.plain: Memorizing the homework instead of learning the subject: perfect on practice examples, lost on new ones.
overfitting.definition: When a model captures noise and quirks of the training set rather than the underlying pattern, so training loss keeps improving while test performance gets worse. The central occupational disease of ML; regularization, more data, and simpler models are the treatments.

underfitting.plain: The model is too simple to catch the pattern at all — a straight line through a clearly curved trend.
underfitting.definition: When a model lacks the capacity (or training) to capture the real structure, performing poorly even on the training set. The cure is a richer model, better features, or longer training.

hyperparameter.plain: A setting the human picks before training starts — training tunes the parameters, you tune the hyperparameters.
hyperparameter.definition: Configuration values not learned from data: learning rate, model size, number of training steps, regularization strength. Chosen by experiment, usually judged on a validation set.

neural-network.plain: Many simple weighted-sum units stacked in layers, with gentle bends between them — enough to learn shapes no straight line can.
neural-network.definition: A model built from layers of linear transformations (weights and biases) separated by nonlinear activation functions. Trained end-to-end with gradient descent via backpropagation. The foundation of deep learning.
